videos.title: Couldn’t afford a house. Now he builds his own underground Missouri home
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
Kyle Yoder couldn't afford to buy a home, so he's been building one himself — by hand, with earth, rubble, and reclaimed materials — on a shared piece of land in northern Missouri.

His first structure was the Gnome Dome: an 80-square-foot earthbag igloo he rescued from demolition after it was aband

videos.title: Was told not to build underground. Ravaging tornado proved him right
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
When Tim and Monica open the front door of their underground home buried beneath a Missouri hillside, they step into a world most people can't fathom. Only one corner of the structure is visible. Everything else has disappeared into the earth.

Fifty years ago, architect and builder Dennis Spencer p

videos.title: Living 35 ft underground: inside a preserved Titan II nuclear missile silo
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
For decades, four missileers at a time lived 35 feet underground in this Titan II command complex outside Tucson, Arizona - working shifts that took them even deeper, 145 feet down into the earth. It tested the limits of both body and mind.

When the Cold War ended, nearly every missile silo in the

videos.title: Family wanted to start over. They're building opt-in village for anyone to apply
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
Sarah and her family of 5 ditched the city grid for a radically self-sufficient life 45 minutes down a dirt road in northern Arizona. They're the first residents of Coslor Cove—an off-grid cooperative where 200 families are building true independence in community.

The town builders didn't start fro

videos.title: Leaves rat race, digs himself remote earthen home with underground rotunda
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
Sammy left his office job in Ohio to teach himself a completely different way of building. He moved to rural Arizona, bought cheap land, and started digging for material and then built part of his own earthbag adobe home directly into one of his earthen quarries.

He's created a 2-story dome half-bu

videos.title: Couple builds homestead of 12 earthen domes linked like private village
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
Nicolette and Ian built 12 earthen domes in 12 months in the middle of the Chihuahuan desert — no construction experience required, no contractor, and no material more exotic than the dirt under their feet.

Their method is superadobe — long tubes of earth coiled upward, layer by layer, barbed wire

videos.title: Tall couple's Big small home: how 1 space gets infinite configurations
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description: In Scott and Susan Burbank's tiny house, almost everything moves — even the kitchen, just not the kitchen sink. Cabinets roll. Counters roll. The kitchen rolls from indoors to outdoors and back. The floors are paper, which is what lets it all slide. The result: a 600-square-foot home that reconfigur

videos.title: 5 years building private village out of free dirt for family of 6
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
After 5 years of traveling full-time in a tiny home, this family of 6 realized they couldn’t go back to a “normal” life—so they bought 11 acres of raw desert in Cochise County, Arizona and started building their own off-grid village from the ground up… using the dirt beneath their feet.

Jonathan an

videos.title: Veterans buy dirt-cheap land to build off-grid coliving homestead
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
On 4 acres of inexpensive desert land in Arizona, Kenny Quinn—a veteran turned earth builder—helped create a radically affordable cohousing compound with people he had just met.

Using little money but a lot of ingenuity, they built their homes from the earth itself. With hyperadobe (open-weave bags

videos.title: It all started with 28 homes. A walkable food-forest made it a village oasis
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
In Tucson's western foothills, 28 households have spent 28 years building something that most neighborhoods have lost — a place where knowing your neighbors isn't incidental, it's the whole point: 

The Milagro Cohousing community is a place where kids can roam freely because the cars park at the ed

videos.title: Rebuilding abandoned giant Earthship into 3-story, off-grid home ecosystem
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
What does it take to rebuild an abandoned giant off-grid Earthship into a fully working 3-story home?

About 45 minutes from the nearest grocery store—and down roads that become impassable when it rains—Chris and Willy are living in a home that has to function entirely on its own. 

When they bought

videos.title: 30 years fine-tuning micro-homestead oasis: nothing missing, little extra
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
For nearly 30 years, David and Pearl Omick have been fine-tuning a tiny, portable home in the Sonoran Desert—an 8×16-foot cottage that provides what they need and little more. 

Built in just three weeks for about $6,000 using simple, widely available materials, the structure was designed to be affo

videos.title: Nordic homestead near Russian border: couple's no-bank, no-phone life
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
"I've only worked for pay for 3 weeks in my entire life. I'm 60." Yet Lasse Nordlund has never stopped hand-building since he began around 40 years ago.

At 63° north in the forests of Finnish Karelia (for comparison, Anchorage, Alaska, is 61.2° N), Lasse Nordlund has spent decades refining an exper

videos.title: He lived on the street. Now turns raw dryland into frugal homestead
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
After being laid off with just $6,000 in savings — and having experienced homelessness — John started over. He left Utah and moved to northern Arizona, where land was still dirt cheap in the middle of cattle country. 

There, he bought 2.5 acres of raw dryland for $1,800. The property wasn’t scenic

videos.title: 4-generation family builds container fourplex atop giant missile silo
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
After purchasing a Titan II Missile Silo Complex in Arizona—decommissioned in 1983 and buried under concrete and dirt for more than 30 years—A Missouri couple packed up their lives and moved into a 5th wheel on the property. 

With grandma, their children, spouses, and grandkids all on board, they s

videos.title: Engineer's underground dome home blends into desert like living organism
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
In 1983, engineer Greg Reinhart bought a patch of raw land in Arizona’s Sonoran Desert and began carving out a home beneath it. Inspired by the animals around him, Greg chose to build an underground, earth-sheltered dome that would use the desert itself as insulation.

Using a kit from Earth Systems

videos.title: They lived 3 years in a basement. Then built their dream backyard home
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
For 3 years, Mai Tran and Le Pham lived in a 300-square-foot basement beneath the house they’d just bought in the Bay Area. No yard. No car. Very few possessions.

By renting out the main house and living as simply as possible, the architect couple saved enough to build what they really wanted: a 63

videos.title: Couple buys whole ghost village, tells others to join & fix their own ruin
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description: On a freezing winter day in northern Spain, we arrived in Bárcena de Bureba, a ghost village in the remote, depopulated hills of the Burgos province. For decades, this quiet place (perched among fields and crisscrossed by a pristine river) lay abandoned, its stone houses crumbling, roofs collapsed,

videos.title: Military couple turns forsaken mountain ruin into off-grid family oasis
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
After leaving Navy careers behind—Maartje (18 years) and Clemens (22 years)—this couple packed their lives into 8 suitcases, sold or gave away nearly everything they owned, and moved with their two daughters to rural Spain to take on a crumbling stone ruin. 

Over the next two years, they poured all

videos.title: Quitting 9-to-5 to build genius off-grid homestead, permitted & loan-free
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description: Five years ago, Mathieu Munsch walked away from the “normal” script— rent, bills, loans, a 40-hour workweek (35 in France)— and headed for a sloping meadow in northeast France to build something radically simple: a small home made from the earth under his feet, wood from nearby, and straw bales from

videos.title: Got factory ruin. Now builds Nordic prefab homes with industrial precision
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
Twenty years ago, VIPP owner Jette Egelund bought an old pencil factory on Copenhagen’s Islands Brygge for next to nothing — less than the price of an apartment in what was then a rundown part of town — and moved in. 

Inside, the beginnings of an iconic design brand were almost comically modest. It

videos.title: Earned $700/month as mayor in Silicon Valley. A backyard cottage became home
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
When Susan Landry became mayor of Campbell, California, she took a pay cut — earning about $700 a month to serve one of the most expensive regions in the country. The job required her to live in the city, but on that salary, moving wasn’t an option. A small backyard cottage became her lifeline.

Tha

videos.title: Turning stone ruin & underground cistern into convertible home to slow down
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
The ancient philosopher Diogenes chose to live in a giant clay pot to strip life down to its essentials.

On Greece’s rugged Mani Peninsula, Elena has taken that idea even further—turning a forgotten underground rainwater cistern into a serene, self-sufficient home designed not to impress, but to sl

videos.title: Got evicted. Then he turned abandoned homestead into troll wonderland 🧌
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
Trash artist Thomas Dambo has transformed an old farm into a whimsical homestead for his family and the dozens of makers who work alongside him. 

Just outside Copenhagen, this imaginative village where nearly every structure, pathway, and piece of furniture is crafted from other people’s discarded

videos.title: Couple raises family in self-sufficient homestead... inside a greenhouse
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
Sylvia, who works for the Swedish military, and Johan, an IT professional, built a family home fully enclosed in a greenhouse as a way to live with nature instead of resisting it. They called it Naturhus Torpadal.

Sylvia grew up on a farm; Johan grew up in the city. The greenhouse became their comp

videos.title: Hidden caves, stone hut & food forest: stealth homestead walkthrough
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description: After years of looking for a piece of simple land—a place we could learn from rather than develop—we finally came across a listing for a plot tucked into the hills near our country home outside Barcelona. Technically, the land is unbuildable. but the listing mentioned a small stone shepherd’s hut an

videos.title: Was told 'You can’t build underground.’ Got dream buried dome fully permitted
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
Brad was told, “You can’t build underground.”

He proved them wrong, self-building the buried dome he had envisioned & getting it permitted against all odds. He realized his dream at a cost in materials of around $80,000. 

Brad and Julia built their buried dome home by carving directly into a steep

videos.title: He built underground maze of light-filled earth homes in CA Sierras
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description: Twenty years ago, Zach and Allison Anderson began digging into a hillside on their property in Grass Valley, California, to escape the heat, cold, and fire risk above ground. What started as a simple experiment has grown into a labyrinth of domed rooms, tunnels, and courtyards — a cluster of undergr

videos.title: Turning suburban backyard into Bronze Age paradise: year-round food & natural lake
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
On an ordinary suburban lot in Sebastopol, California, Erik Ohlsen and his family grow nearly everything they eat—fruits, vegetables, nuts, herbs, even their own medicine. 

Their home is surrounded by edible forest, a rain-fed pond for swimming, and habitats that attract foxes, frogs, and dragonfli

videos.title: Couple’s traditional underground home hides in magical Nordic forest
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
In an enchanted forest in Småland, Sweden, Marianna and Anders Agetorp live on a fairytale-like homestead surrounded by moss, boulders, and stories of trolls. 

On their land, they re-built their own 100-year-old backstuga — a traditional earth-sheltered home dug into the hillside and so perfectly b

videos.title: They found abandoned pirate fortress & turned it into dream hideout home
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
It looks like a forgotten pirate fortress, straight out of The Count of Monte Cristo or Game of Thrones: a stone stronghold perched on the sea, complete with a lookout tower, private island, and a nearby lighthouse. 

Known as Teloneio, “the customs house,” this 18th-century outpost has been transfo

videos.title: How Finland fixed homelessness while the US fails: home vs. shelter
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
Meet the American idealists inspired by Finland’s model to end homelessness.

It all started when they noticed something odd from a conventional US perspective: a European country had managed to tackle homelessness, even the most difficult cases.

How come? The answer was simple enough: get people i

videos.title: He created prefab kit to self-build underground homes on a budget
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
Dave has spent the past decade refining ways to build affordable underground homes. We last visited him to see his Quonset hut–style earth homes, and now he’s back with a new prototype: a prefab “hobbit house” that anyone can build on a budget.

Next door to his dome home, Dave has constructed the f

videos.title: Starting over in Ghost Town: they found perfect village to escape 9-to-5
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
Shaniko, Oregon — once the “Wool Capital of the World” — went bust more than a century ago and with its hotels shuttered, storefronts abandoned, and population dwindled to just a few dozen, it nearly disappeared, but today although only 33 residents remain, the town is slowly staging a comeback.

At

videos.title: Couple's off-grid living on Nordic island II: preparing for winter at 63° N
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
At 63° North, life on a small Finnish island brings long winters, frozen lakes, and a rhythm deeply tied to nature. In this second part of our visit with Klaus and Johanna, we explore how they navigate the challenges—and joys—of island life through the coldest months.

Winter brings periods when the

videos.title: Couple builds fulfilling life & homestead on abandoned Nordic island
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
At 63° North — where the midnight sun lingers and winters test endurance at 35 below — a young couple has made a life on a once-forgotten island in Finland.

Ten years ago, Klaus purchased this abandoned 1.5-hectare isle for a modest price. It had been left untouched for 42 years, with only a collap

videos.title: Leaving tech to build wild-canyon homestead: Watch Duty creator's life
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
John Mills' story is part survival guide, part tech revolution, and part experiment in how to live with the land in an era of growing wildfires.

In 2019, John Mills left San Francisco to start an off-grid homestead in Northern California, moving into a hay-bale house on 170 acres. A lifelong coder

videos.title: 68 & starting fresh: building DIY 2-cabin dream homestead from scratch
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
At 68, Jeannette Belliveau sold her Baltimore home and moved to rural Idaho to build her dream homestead from the ground up. 

With no prior building experience, she relied on YouTube tutorials and the help of neighbors and even local high school students to construct two arched cabins on her 16 acr

videos.title: Turning giant Cold War missile silo into 50-ton-door underground home
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description: In the early 1990s, retired engineer David McIntyre bought what most people would see as an empty underground shell: a decommissioned Atlas E missile silo just outside the tiny town of Sprague, Washington (population 525). Where others saw a relic of the Cold War, McIntyre saw the challenge of makin

videos.title: 35 years underground: Dan Price's living free on tranquil river meadow
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
For 35 years, Dan Price has lived underground in a tiny “hobbit hole” tucked into a quiet Oregon mountain meadow. When we first filmed him a decade ago, he showed us his 80-square-foot home and office where he once drew and printed Moonlight Chronicles, his hand-illustrated zine.

Now 68, Dan still

videos.title: 6 friends, 6 units, 1 lot: each owns their dream home for $800/month
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description: In a town where average rent is $1,400, six single adults became homeowners for just $10,000 each—and now pay $800/month to cover the mortgage and maintenance on their six one-bedroom homes. The C Street Co-op in Springfield, Oregon (the real-life inspiration for The Simpsons) is home to empty-neste

videos.title: Dream Domestead uses water & geothermal for all-season paradise in Spain
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description: This homestead includes two handcrafted natural homes, a geodesic dome greenhouse built from local stone, a hand-dug stream, two natural swimming ponds, a bamboo maze, and a thriving permaculture garden and orchard with over 2,000 plants. It’s a place where the home doesn’t just shelter you—it feeds

videos.title: She got ancient hermit tower, cave & chapel for the price of a car
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description: In the wild, stony hills of the Mani Peninsula—once home to pirates, hermits, and feuding clans— Elena Zervoudakis stumbled upon a ruin so isolated it could only be reached by foot, up a stone path carved into the mountainside. Her friends urged her to spend the money on something more sensible —lik

videos.title: Family's self-built greenhouse home in farm country has indoor orchard 🌳
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description: When Roja Brimalm and Johan Holmstedt set out to build a home for their family on the windswept plains of rural Sweden, they weren’t builders or architects — Johan is a mechanic and Roja a chef — but they had a vision: to create a self-sustaining bubble of life that could thrive even in the bitter c

videos.title: NYC microstudio transforms on-demand with wall-embedded furniture
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
When Emmy-nominated actress Laverne Cox ("Orange Is the New Black") bought her first home—a 634-square-foot studio in Manhattan she first toured while filming "Inventing Anna"—she knew she wanted a space that could feel as expansive and intentional as her life. 

Robert Garneau's Pivot Apartment: ht

videos.title: Expandable home with loop-filtered water is indoor/outdoor game changer
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
Experiencing MO.CA gives one the feeling of entering a new type of mobile living: it's flexible, indoor/outdoor, and entirely off-grid, with the ability to clean its water so one can take endless showers (thanks to a greywater cleaning loop-system running on solar).

As an experiment in how much liv

videos.title: Thrifty couple grows all their food in their food-forest backyard
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
When Kenta learned that 90% of Hawai‘i’s food—and nearly all of its carbohydrates—are imported, he began to rethink how he wanted to live. Seeking greater food security, he and his wife Julia left the city and moved to a quiet corner of the Big Island to try building a more self-sufficient life.

In

videos.title: Couple encases brick farm inside greenhouse for indoor/outdoor all year
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
On the windswept island of Mors in Northern Denmark, Anna Marie Madsen and Rasmus Møller have reimagined rural living by encasing a modest brick home inside a 247-square-meter greenhouse—creating a light-filled, sun-warmed refuge that stays connected to nature year-round.

To keep costs low, the cou

videos.title: Building budget homestead little by little in worthless, remote (is)land
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
Thirty years ago, Vici stood on the rocky hillsides of Serifos and imagined a home made of stone — like the traditional dwellings that once dotted the island. No one thought it was possible on her budget.

At the time, stone construction was seen as outdated, impractical, and prohibitively difficult

videos.title: Turning dark & narrow apartment into luminous, plant-filled home oasis
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
In the heart of Barcelona's Eixample district, a long, narrow apartment—typical of the city’s early 20th-century “shotgun” floor plans—has been transformed into a flowing, plant-filled home-studio for a couple by the young architects of Twobo.

Dubbed Casa del Ebanista ("House of the Cabinetmaker")

videos.title: Life-Changing Homes for healing, personal growth & starting over 👉🌱
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
Life-Changing Homes is a book for people in transition—those starting over, downsizing, dreaming bigger, or simply rethinking what "home" can mean. 

Whether you’re looking for inspiration to build your own off-grid cabin, move into a co-living space, or transform your existing home into a place of

videos.title: He excavated earth-sheltered home of dry stone into rugged seacliff
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
Dug into the mountainside and built with the very earth excavated to make space for it, Xerolithi House is a natural, earth-sheltered home—designed both as a refuge from the elements and as a seamless extension of its environment.

Inspired by the region’s dry stone walls—called xerolithies—and buil

videos.title: Our book is out: LIFE-CHANGING HOMES is for you
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen

videos.title: Family-style ecovillage is co-housing at heart of huge food-forest 🥗
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
In the lush rainforest of Hawai’i’s Big Island, a small group of people has created a way of life that’s rooted in the land. At La’akea Community, residents live off-grid, grow their own food, and build their own homes using some materials harvested from the property. 

With a thriving food forest a

videos.title: Tetris founder's family village is collapse-proof, remote offgrid-topia
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
High in the hills of Hawaii’s Big Island, Henk Rogers—best known for bringing Tetris to the world—is taking on a new kind of challenge: building a fully off-grid life. On his 32-acre ranch, he’s growing his own food, producing his own energy, and working to protect Hawaii’s future.

At his main home

videos.title: 91-year-old American granny revives ghost village, brings lost paradise back to life
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
When Rebecca left her mountain village on the Mani (one of the three peninsulas in Greece's southern Peloponnese) in 1951, she was a teenager escaping postwar poverty and a struggling local economy for a new life in America. 

Now 91, she spends half the year back in the old family café, transformed

videos.title: He escaped Iron Curtain on a flying go-kart. Now lives in 1-person Ghost Town
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
Ivo Zdarsky lives alone in Lucin, Utah—a ghost town so remote it barely registers on most maps. His home is a metal airplane hangar, surrounded by dirt runways he graded himself. There are no neighbors, no cell service, no paved roads—just one man, his plane, and a vast, empty landscape.

It’s a qui

videos.title: Secret Underground Home vanishes into Greek hills like a hidden passage
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
Just beyond the olive groves of Sikamino, Greece, a jagged seam in the earth quietly declares its presence. In a narrow, sloping field an hour outside Athens, a home doesn’t sit on the land—it emerges from it like the half-open eye of a giant resting beneath the hillside.

For Greek architect Tilema

videos.title: Friends pool money to build Scandinavian ecovillage in self-owned factory
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
In the small Danish town of Ebeltoft, 160 friends did something most people only dream about: they pooled their money to buy a sprawling, decommissioned steel factory. 

The building, cold and cavernous, wasn’t much to look at at first. But for this group, it represented something rare: a chance to

videos.title: How family of 7 lived simply in tiny stone home by the sea in Greece
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
On the quiet Greek island of Serifos, in the once-bustling mining village of Megalo Livadi, sits a small whitewashed house perched just above the water. It was here, in this humble two-room home, that Giannis, a retired naval engineer now in his seventies, grew up alongside his four siblings.

One r

videos.title: Couple's alleyway home feels like oasis farm in the middle of DC
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description: Tucked away in a Capitol Hill alley, Jack Becker and Maddie Hoagland-Hanson’s Brown House is a lush oasis in the heart of D.C., meant to provide much more than habitation: its levels and indoor/outdoor permeability help Becker, an architect, and Hoagland-Hanson, a horticulturist, rest, play, and wor

videos.title: Couple builds own home & farm paradise with little money + inspiring work
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
In rural Denmark, Tycho Holcomb and Karoline Nolsø Aaen have crafted a life of independence, resilience, and deep connection to nature. 

Using only hand tools and solar power, they built their off-grid home for just $30,000—freeing themselves from debt while creating a nearly bill-free existence.

videos.title: Manhattan telescopic apartment grows with sliding walls & hidden bedroom
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
Determined to make their small apartment work for their growing family, one couple turned their home into a shape-shifting marvel. 

They wanted to stay in their beloved space but needed more functionality—so they enlisted architect Robert Garneau, a master of transforming apartments. 

His solution

videos.title: Couple's own paradise in rural WA: yurt, cabin & barndominium homestead
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
After years in the city, Michael Lerner and his wife, Amiee, were ready for a change. Selling their Seattle home—where Michael had built an ADU from scratch—they set out for the countryside, where they wanted to start afresh and get in sync with the land.

They found the perfect spot an hour and a h

videos.title: Building dream home out of railroad bridge & 1905 craftsman bungalow
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
In a city that churns through its past at breakneck speed—where bungalows vanish overnight— Steve Pallrand has built a home from the rubble. He calls it Casa Zero.

Built almost entirely from salvaged materials—redwood from a demolished 1905 Craftsman, beams from a long-forgotten railroad bridge, br

videos.title: Special Operations vet commutes by Flying Car from rural off-grid home
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
In the remote mountains of Washington’s North Cascades, Tim Lum commutes in a way few can imagine—by flying car. 

From his off-grid home in Twisp, the retired Air Force pararescueman pilots his all-electric BlackFly, an ultralight aircraft that lifts off from his property and cruises at 62 miles pe

videos.title: Veteran's underground home in Maine Homestead is affordable little marvel
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
Jim's idea of happiness includes a 1-mile zipline that descends an entire hill and crosses a lake to get from one corner of the property to his house in rural Maine.

In the 1970s, Vietnam veteran Jim stepped off the beaten path—way off. With little money but an abundance of vision, he bought 600 ac

videos.title: $15K remote homestead: bridging inexpensive kit homes in Scandinavian Arctic
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
In Norway’s remote far north, where the wind can rattle walls and the winter light is fleeting, Kjetil Ingvar Berge has built a home that forces him to step outside—literally. 

The artist, drawn to the idea of simple living, purchased an abandoned 100-year-old kit home for the astonishingly low pri

videos.title: Kristie Wolfe turns ruin into pirate paradise + old treasure beneath 🏴‍☠️
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
When Kristie Wolfe first saw the ship-shaped home—half of it slipping into a small Salmon, Idaho lake—she knew anyone else would knock it down, but she couldn’t let that happen. 

Someone had built this fantastical house in the 1960s, seemingly just for fun, long before social media turned whimsical

videos.title: Could Quiet Van be healthier than Home? His story might surprise you
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
In Southern California, where wildfires and housing shortages displaced countless residents, alternative living options like RVs and vans are more than a trend—they’re a lifeline. 

TIMELINE: 
—0:05 Wildfires in California: how some homeowners live on RVs while they rebuild after fire. Our friend Jo

videos.title: L.A. startup speeds up housing with fireproof prefab homes 🛡️🔥
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
We’ve been following the journey of Cover, a Los Angeles-based prefab home builder, almost since their inception a decade ago. 

Their goal has always been ambitious: to rethink how homes are built by combining precision engineering, factory production, and modern design. 

Over the years, we’ve wat

videos.title: How entire village runs fully off-grid on small hydropower & solar
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
Thirty years ago, a group of permaculture experts purchased 329 acres of degraded land in Western North Carolina with a vision: to restore the landscape while creating a new model of communal living. 

What began as an experiment in living well off the land has grown into one of the nation’s oldest

videos.title: 4 generations, 4 homes, 1 lot: Vancouver family builds own private neighborhood
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
To help their kids afford housing in the Vancouver area, John and Kathleen Higgins demolished their family home of three decades to build 4 homes on the same lot for their kids, grandkids, and 94-year-old great-grannie.

videos.title: She built underground cabin in remote Arctic for $200 as cozy Stealth Home
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
Franciska Eliassen built an underground earth home inspired by traditional Norwegian sod huts to live simply and sustainably from what the land could offer. 

Using salvaged materials—driftwood, discarded planks, and secondhand windows—she spent only a couple of hundred dollars on hardware and water

videos.title: Scandinavian ecovillage rethinks homeownership: no mortgages, no waste
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
Twenty-two years ago, a group of friends bought a very affordable cornfield in rural Denmark and set out to build homes that were both debt-free and waste-free. 

What began as a bold experiment with 13 families soon transformed the land into the eco-village of Friland (which means "Freeland"), a ti

videos.title: 12th-Century Farmhouse: The Sounds of Simple Living 🎶
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:

Step into the sounds of solitude on a 12th-century Catalan farmhouse, where architect Marc Valero embraces the rhythms of the countryside. From the whisper of ivy on stone walls to the gentle lapping of water at the restored mill pond, every s

videos.title: Young architect's life in 12th-century Masia: see original furnishings
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
Nestled in a serene valley, architect Marc Valero lives alone in a 12th-century watermill and farmhouse, a catalan masia immersed in the rhythms of nature and the slow quiet of a life shaped by the land’s enduring beauty.

The farmhouse, Can Capsec, embraces simplicity, a testament to the passage of

videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
Full-length video here 👉 https://youtu.be/cHryf1UCUgs ☺️

Ignacio Morente bought a crumbling, century-old warehouse near Barcelona and turned it into a four-story oasis. With the help of family and friends, he built a dream home blending ancient and modern design. The highlight? An underground Roman

videos.title: Whole ecovillage fits inside giant greenhouse: walkable, weatherproof
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
Thirty years ago, a group of friends in British Columbia set out to create a new kind of ecovillage—and they built it under glass. Located 30 miles south of Vancouver, WindSong is Canada’s first cohousing community, designed with pedestrian streets covered by a greenhouse-style roof. 

The goal was

videos.title: An ecovillage inside a greenhouse
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
What happens when you design a community that’s protected from the elements— year-round? Welcome to WindSong, Canada’s first cohousing community, where 34 homes are linked by a greenhouse-style roof, keeping residents connected through rain, snow, and sunshine. 🌦️

This is a trailer for a longer sto

videos.title: Her family farms flowers. Her flower pot home is coziness masterclass
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
Whitney Hansen and her mother Krista turned an unconventional idea into reality: a 24-foot-tall flower pot-shaped house on their family’s 65-year-old farm in southern Idaho. 

Inspired by Krista’s retirement dream of starting a you-pick flower farm, the 436-square-foot structure is both a tribute to

videos.title: Arizona's autonomous home: entirely off-grid, no hookups needed
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
Brian Stark’s Sparkboxes can add a fully functional home to your backyard—or a remote location—in just one day, with no utility hookups required and in record time.

With an incinerating toilet, a two-month water supply, and enough solar power to run everything, these homes are ready to live in the

videos.title: Northern Scandinavia's forgotten lifestyle: family of 14 in remote cabin
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
In the far north, where life revolves around the sea, Birger’s father grew up in a world without cars, where reaching the nearest store required hours of rowing. Self-sufficiency wasn’t a choice—it was a necessity. 

During the long and harsh winter, they had to put up with the fact that there wasn'

videos.title: "Live from California" legend shares SuperAging secret, DIY-building tips 🛹
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description: At 89, Lloyd Kahn has been building his own homes for over six decades, crafting spaces that include a few domes, at least one tower, and plenty of hand-split shingles. He's documented his builds, as well as the makeshift homes of thousands of alternative builders all over the US and the world, with

videos.title: Building ecovillage of storm-proof homes made of bone-like biomaterial
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
On a rural property on the slopes of California's Sierra Nevada mountains, Morgan Bierschenk and his team have been refining a durable, mineral-like material to build sturdy dome homes with a particular matte-ceramic finish. They have called their modular dome homes Geoships.

videos.title: Turns warehouse into dream home with underground living + Roman bath 🏛️
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description: Ignacio Morente bought a dilapidated warehouse and, with help from family and friends, he built his dream home, complete with an underground living space and a buried Roman bath, creating a bioclimatic haven for days that are either too hot or cold. videos.title: Tenant couldn't afford L.A. An alleyway studio made him stay
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
More and more families living in urban and suburban setups across California are slowly turning their single-family homes into lots with two dwelling units thanks to secondary suits (ADUs).

Jefferson Schierbeek and Su Addison transformed a rundown LA garage into a new structure with the same footpr

videos.title: Couple transforms oil tank into panoramic home on tiny fjord Island
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
In the tiny Scandinavian island of Skrova north of the Arctic Circle, Martin Otterbeck and Agnete Brun bought an old, rusty oil tank that once held 200,000 liters (53,000 gallons) of diesel and transformed it into a modern home with panoramic 360-degree views of the surrounding Norwegian fjord.

videos.title: Built off-grid homestead. Then became Fermented Foods' go-to expert
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
When Sandor Katz moved to rural Tennessee from Manhattan after a health crisis, he had no experience with gardening nor fermentation (besides a love of pickles), but with his first bumper crop of cabbage, he turned to fermentation (in this case sauerkraut) for preservation. 

Encouraged to eat more

videos.title: Turns forsaken WW2 bunker into perfect Arctic underground cabin
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
After discovering an abandoned World War II bunker while on a hike in northern Norway, Henrik Lande Andersen spent two years transforming this former German WW2 lookout into an overnight refuge open to all. 

Over the course of two years, Henrik made the hike from town - the only way to access the s

videos.title: Friends couldn't afford rent. Pooled together to buy homes instead
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
Phil Levin and Kristen Berman wanted to live near their friends, but they didn’t want to sacrifice their privacy, so they started their own intentional community where you can choose your neighbors and eat together but still have your own home. They called it Radish.

Today, on their one-third-acre

videos.title: He quit his job to build dream cliffside hamlet in Appalachia
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
While on a bike ride outside of Asheville (North Carolina), Doug and Jen Mielke fell in love with an overgrown high mountain pasture and spent the next 10 years convincing the owner to sell. When they finally purchased the land 15 years ago, they began to recreate shelters suited to the place.

videos.title: How entire villages lived in just 1 building in remote Far North
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
For thousands of years survival in Norway's countryside, where farms were miles from each other, meant complete self-sufficiency. Farmsteads were like small villages with several families living together and building their homes from logs and turf.

At Oslo's Norwegian Folk Museum, entire farmsteads

videos.title: Family builds dream-like village of dugout homes into remote SoCal hill
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
Homes are square, they said. Polymath artist James Hubbell built 100%-curvy Hobbit Village to prove them wrong 🐚

In 1958, James and Anne Hubbell bought cheap land in the Cuyamaca Mountains east of San Diego to build a home with their own hands using materials from the land. Over the next several de

videos.title: The lot fit 9 McMansions. They built 44 small homes for locals instead
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description: Maggie and John Randolph kept losing employees priced out of the New Hampshire coast, so they got to support local workers by building a pocket neighborhood of tiny homes, helping them avoid long commutes or housing issues. The lot was originally zoned for 9 luxury homes, but they took advantage of

videos.title: Guide to the Good Life from architect-farmer amid redwoods: 55 years off-grid
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
At age 92, after 55 years living off the land, Charles Bello still spends long days growing his own food and managing his off-grid infrastructure. To him, this continuous connection to the land isn't a sacrifice but a gift that allows him to experience daily wonder amid nature.

videos.title: Family wraps homestead in greenhouse to warm up & grow food all year
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description: When Margit-Kristine Solibakke Klev, an architect working near the Norwegian town of Kongsberg, decided to design a home for her family, she enclosed the build in a commercial greenhouse kit to endure harsh winters at mild temperatures and to grow food all year thanks to a productive indoor garden.

videos.title: Viking longhouse above Arctic Circle is protective half-buried dwelling
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description: Three hundred kilometers north of the Polar Circle, on the Lofoten Island of Vestvågøya, a farmer discovered the remains of the largest Viking Era longhouse ever uncovered. videos.title: Young couple builds cozy home with indoor garden for $30K (Denmark)
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description:
When David Westervik left the Norwegian coastal city of Bergen to study architecture in Aarhus, Denmark, he decided to live in a small sailboat to save money and sail across the North Atlantic.

videos.title: They turn old trucks into 100% solar-powered homes on wheels: No need of 🔌
videos.author: Kirsten Dirksen
videos.description: Brett and Kira Belan have spent the past decade transforming old vehicles into solar-powered campers.
